reflection.failure_analysis: Agent succeeded by correctly reporting absent lab values and applying protocol logic only after confirming no results were found.
action_guidelines: [P1] When no lab results are found for required tests, explicitly state that the values are missing and conclude no protocol action is needed.
[P1] Do not infer or fabricate lab values; report absence as a valid outcome.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent searched for active DVT prophylaxis orders but failed to identify relevant anticoagulant orders. Instead of creating a new order, it should have recognized that existing orders already met the requirement, leading to incorrect action.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent incorrectly concluded zero DVT prophylaxis orders because it did not verify anticoagulant medications beyond heparin/LMWH/DOACs or check alternative drug classes used for DVT prophylaxis.
action_guidelines: [P1] When checking for DVT prophylaxis, search for all anticoagulant orders including heparin, enoxaparin, warfarin, DOACs, and other agents listed in local protocols.
[P1] If no matching orders found initially, verify the patient's medication list against a comprehensive list of DVT prophylaxis agents before concluding absence.
[P1] Before creating a new order, double-check that no existing active order covers the intended indication using broad medication category searches.

kept: [P1] When retrieving lab values, query for each test separately using its specific LOINC code.
[P1] When querying observations over a time window, use a date range slightly larger than required to account for data entry delays.
[P1] If no observations are returned for a specific vital sign code, try related alternative codes (e.g., for heart rate, try 8885-5) before concluding absence.
[P1] When checking vaccination status, first search the Immunization resource with no code filter to capture all records, then filter programmatically for the vaccine of interest.
[P1] If primary search returns no results, try alternative resource types (e.g., Immunization vs Procedure, MedicationRequest) before concluding absence.
[P1] Apply protocol logic only after confirming all requested lab values have been searched and accounted for.
[P1] Avoid applying date filters in initial searches for vaccination dates; retrieve all records first then filter programmatically.
[P1] Compare each lab value to the protocol threshold to determine required action.
[P1] Before acting on absence of a record, confirm absence through at least two independent data sources.
[P1] When querying medications, use standard coded identifiers (e.g., RxNorm) for the medication, not free-text names.
[P1] Document both found and missing lab results explicitly in the summary.

[P1] When checking for indwelling medical devices (e.g., catheters, lines), first retrieve all Device records for the patient without a code filter, then filter results programmatically and calculate dwell time from insertion date.
[P1] When querying vital signs, always use the standardized LOINC code for the specific measurement (e.g., heart rate = 8867-4), not free-text or non-standard codes.
[P1] If an initial search attempt fails due to invalid parameters, correct the query and retry before concluding absence.
[P1] Before acting, verify that all required FHIR search parameters are correct and complete.
[P1] When searching for a procedure date, query all available data sources (e.g., radiology reports, structured data, free-text notes) before concluding it does not exist.
[P1] Retrieve all relevant lab values for the patient before applying protocol rules.
[P1] Do not assume a specific procedure code is correct; verify by inspecting all available procedure records first.
[P1] If a lab result is not found in initial search, verify absence by checking all available lab resources (e.g., Observation) before concluding missing.
[P1] If a specific code search yields no results, re-verify by searching with alternative codes or terms before concluding absence.
[P1] For time-sensitive device checks (e.g., >48 hours), retrieve all device records without date filters, then calculate duration programmatically.

[P1] When checking for a specific device or procedure, first retrieve all relevant procedures for the patient using a broad search without code filters, then narrow down only after reviewing all available results.
[P1] Before concluding zero orders, verify search parameters (patient ID, medication codes, status) and retry with a broad query.
[P1] When searching for medications, use a broad query without restrictive filters first to confirm the patient has any medication records.
[P1] If a vital sign query fails, verify the code used is a valid LOINC code before retrying.

reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because it used a narrow code filter prematurely instead of first retrieving all device records to check for catheter presence and dwell time.

[P1] If no results from a narrow code query, broaden to all records of the relevant resource type before concluding absence.

reflection.failure_analysis: Agent prematurely filtered by specific codes without confirming those codes represent the target procedure, and performed a combined code search instead of searching broadly or using confirmed codes.
action_guidelines: [P1] When searching for a procedure, first retrieve all Procedure resources for the patient without code filters, then review the list to identify the target procedure by display name or other attributes.
[P1] Do not assume unknown codes map to a specific procedure; verify code-to-procedure mapping from existing records before using codes in queries.
[P1] When uncertain about procedure codes, search by patient alone and inspect results to discover the correct codes before narrowing down.

[P1] If a code-filtered search returns no results, broaden the search to all procedures before concluding absence.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent failed to verify opioid-naloxone pairing for patient S1374652. It made a single broad MedicationRequest query but did not filter for active status, opioid class, or naloxone type, nor did it iterate or create missing orders. Task limit reached without completing verification or prescription.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent performed only one generic search without narrowing to active opioid orders or checking for naloxone co-prescriptions. It lacked a stepwise verification and conditional creation workflow, causing task timeout.
action_guidelines: [P1] When verifying medication pairs (e.g., opioid + naloxone), first query active orders only, then separate into opioid and naloxone lists.
[P1] After identifying active opioids without matching naloxone, immediately create the missing naloxone order without additional lookups.
[P1] For medication safety checks, use status filters (e.g., 'active') and drug class codes in the initial query, not post-query filtering alone.

[P1] When searching for a procedure, start with a broad search without any code filters, then narrow down after reviewing results.
[P1] If a specific code search returns no results, revert to a broad search before concluding absence.

  "action_guidelines": [

  ],
  "tags": ["premature filtering", "code filter", "procedure search", "broad search first"],
  "should_store_episode": true,
  "should_update_semantic": true
}

kept: [P1] Before concluding a vaccine is absent, search at least two independent data sources (e.g., structured Immunization resource, clinical notes, encounter diagnoses).
[P1] When retrieving lab values, query for each test separately using its specific LOINC code, and confirm absence only after querying all required tests.
[P1] Do not switch resources or add code filters until all relevant records from the primary resource have been exhausted.
[P1] When checking for indwelling devices, always start with a broad Device search without code filters, then filter programmatically.
[P1] If primary search returns no results, try alternative resource types (e.g., Immunization vs Procedure, MedicationRequest) before concluding absence.

[P1] When checking for a specific device or procedure, first retrieve all relevant records for the patient using a broad search without code filters, then narrow down only after reviewing all available results.
[P1] Apply protocol logic only after confirming all requested lab values have been searched and accounted for.
[P1] When querying medications, use standard coded identifiers (e.g., RxNorm) for the medication, not free-text names.
[P1] Avoid applying date filters in initial searches for vaccination dates; retrieve all records first then filter programmatically.
[P1] Before acting on absence of a record, confirm absence through at least two independent data sources.
[P1] Compare each lab value to the protocol threshold to determine required action.
[P1] Document both found and missing lab results explicitly in the summary.
[P1] When querying observations over a time window, use a date range slightly larger than required to account for data entry delays.
[P1] If no observations are returned for a specific vital sign code, try related alternative codes (e.g., for heart rate, try 8885-5) before concluding absence.
[P1] When querying vital signs, always use the standardized LOINC code for the specific measurement (e.g., heart rate = 8867-4), not free-text or non-standard codes.
[P1] If an initial search attempt fails due to invalid parameters, correct the query and retry before concluding absence.
[P1] When searching for a procedure date, query all available data sources (e.g., radiology reports, structured data, free-text notes) before concluding it does not exist.
[P1] Retrieve all relevant lab values for the patient before applying protocol rules.
[P1] Before acting, verify that all required FHIR search parameters are correct and complete.
[P1] If a lab result is not found in initial search, verify absence by checking all available lab resources (e.g., Observation) before concluding missing.
[P1] If a specific code search yields no results, re-verify by searching with alternative codes or terms before concluding absence.
[P1] Before concluding zero orders, verify search parameters (patient ID, medication codes, status) and retry with a broad query.
[P1] When searching for medications, use a broad query without restrictive filters first to confirm the patient has any medication records.
[P1] If a vital sign query fails, verify the code used is a valid LOINC code before retrying.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent was tasked with verifying active opioid orders and creating naloxone prescriptions. It searched for specific opioids but found none and failed to complete the verification or action, resulting in an invalid action.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent prematurely concluded absence of opioid orders after a narrow search without confirming the patient had any medication records. It did not perform a broad medication query first to validate the patient's medication list.
action_guidelines: [P1] Before searching for specific medications, first retrieve all active medication orders for the patient using a broad query without drug filters.
[P1] After obtaining all medication records, then filter programmatically for the target drugs (e.g., opioids).
[P1] If no results from a specific drug search, verify the patient has any medication records at all before concluding absence.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent searched for COVID-19 vaccine in Immunization, Procedure, and MedicationRequest but found none. Incorrectly concluded absence means no dose exists, so ordered a booster. Actually, patient had prior COVID-19 vaccine documented elsewhere, making the action premature.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because it did not exhaust all possible data sources for COVID-19 vaccine records before concluding absence. The existing semantic rules already warn against this, but the agent violated [P1] by not searching at least two independent sources thoroughly.
action_guidelines: [P1] When searching for immunization history, query all relevant resource types (Immunization, Procedure, Observation, MedicationAdministration) before concluding absence.
[P1] Do not treat 'no records found' as equivalent to 'no vaccine given'; verify with alternative codes or broader searches.
[P1] If a patient has no structured immunization records, check clinical notes or encounter diagnoses for mention of the vaccine.

reflection.failure_analysis: The agent used an incorrect date range parameter syntax (two separate date parameters instead of a single combined range), causing the query to be rejected.
action_guidelines:

...[truncated]
[P1] Always verify the query syntax against the FHIR API specification before executing to avoid invalid action errors.

reflection_raw:
{
  "episodic_summary": "Agent attempted to query heart rate using LOINC code 8867-4 over a 12-hour window but failed due to an invalid date range format in the FHIR query.",
  "failure_analysis": "The agent used an incorrect date range parameter syntax (two separate date parameters instead of a single combined range), causing the query to be rejected.",
  "action_guidelines": [
    "When specifying a date range in FHIR queries, use a single date parameter with 'ge' and 'le' modifiers combined, e.g., 'date=ge2023-11-07T10:47:00Z&date=le2023-11-07T22:47:00Z' is invalid; instead use 'date=2023-11-07T10:47:00Z--2023-11-07T22:47:00Z'.",
    "Always verify the query syntax against the FHIR API specification before executing to avoid invalid action errors."
  ],
  "tags": ["FHIR query syntax", "date range", "invalid action"],
  "should_store_episode": true,
  "should_update_semantic": true
}

[P1] Always verify the query syntax against the FHIR API specification before executing to avoid invalid action errors.

reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because it applied a strict frequency filter (Q8H) to identify DVT prophylaxis, ignoring that the existing order (Q12H) may still be intended for prophylaxis. It should have checked the order's reasonCode or intent before discarding it.
action_guidelines: [P1] When identifying orders for a specific purpose (e.g., DVT prophylaxis), check the order's reasonCode or intent, not just the drug name or dose frequency.
[P1] Before paginating for more results, verify that the current order's purpose matches the clinical goal using available coded reasons or clinical context.
[P1] If a single order exists but its frequency differs from expected, do not assume it is the wrong type; first confirm its indication via reasonCode or encounter context.

added: [P1] When searching for a procedure, first verify the code exists by querying the terminology server or using a broad search without codes.
removed: [P1] If a code-specific search returns no results, try alternative codes or free-text searches before concluding absence.
[P1] Do not reuse codes from unrelated contexts without confirming they apply to the current patient and task.
kept: [P1] Before concluding a vaccine is absent, search at least two independent data sources (e.g., structured Immunization resource, clinical notes, encounter diagnoses) and use multiple code systems (e.g., CVX, SNOMED, RxNorm).
[P1] If no results are returned from a query, adjust parameters (e.g., broaden date range, remove status filters, verify patient ID) and retry before concluding absence.
[P1] Do not switch resources or add code filters until all relevant records from the primary resource have been exhausted.
[P1] When checking for specific drug classes, use standardized codes (e.g., RxNorm, ATC) and expand search to include both active and historical orders.
[P1] When retrieving lab values, query for each test separately using its specific LOINC code, and confirm absence only after querying all required tests.
[P1] If primary search returns no results, try alternative resource types (e.g., Immunization vs Procedure, MedicationRequest) before concluding absence.
[P1] When checking for indwelling devices, always start with a broad Device search without code filters, then filter programmatically.
[P1] Apply protocol logic only after confirming all requested lab values have been searched and accounted for.
[P1] When querying medications, use standard coded identifiers (e.g., RxNorm) for the medication, not free-text names.
[P1] Avoid applying date filters in initial searches for vaccination dates; retrieve all records first then filter programmatically.
[P1] Compare each lab value to the protocol threshold to determine required action.
[P1] Before concluding data is absent, always execute queries for the specific measurement using its standardized LOINC code.
[P1] Document both found and missing lab results explicitly in the summary.
[P1] If no observations are returned for a specific vital sign code, try related alternative codes (e.g., for heart rate, try 8885-5) before concluding absence.
[P1] If an initial search attempt fails due to invalid parameters, correct the query and retry before concluding absence.
[P1] If a lab result is not found in initial search, verify absence by checking all available lab resources (e.g., Observation) before concluding missing.
[P1] When querying observations over a time window, use a date range slightly larger than required to account for data entry delays.
[P1] Before acting, verify that all required FHIR search parameters are correct and complete.
[P1] When searching for a procedure date, query all available data sources (e.g., radiology reports, structured data, free-text notes) before concluding it does not exist.
[P1] Retrieve all relevant lab values for the patient before applying protocol rules.
[P1] When querying vital signs, always use the standardized LOINC code for the specific measurement (e.g., heart rate = 8867-4), not free-text or non-standard codes.
[P1] If a specific code search yields no results, re-verify by searching with alternative codes or terms before concluding absence.
[P1] Before concluding zero orders, verify search parameters (patient ID, medication codes, status) and retry with a broad query.
[P1] When searching for medications, use a broad query without restrictive filters first to confirm the patient has any medication records.
[P1] If a vital sign query fails, verify the code used is a valid LOINC code before retrying.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent attempted to check for a urinary catheter by searching for Procedure with a specific code and date filter, but used an invalid action format. The search was too narrow and lacked initial broad exploration.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because it directly applied a code filter and date range without first performing a broad procedure search to confirm the presence of any catheter-related records. This violates the principle of exhausting broad searches before applying filters.
action_guidelines: [P1] When checking for indwelling devices, start with a broad Procedure search without code filters to identify all device-related records.
[P1] After obtaining broad results, filter programmatically by relevant codes and date ranges.
[P1] Ensure the request URL format is valid and matches the expected FHIR API structure before sending.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent attempted to verify opioid orders for a patient but only viewed first page of active medications. It recognized need for pagination and requested next page, but action was marked invalid, likely due to incorrect pagination URL or method.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because the pagination request was invalid (malformed URL or missing required parameters), preventing completion of the medication review workflow.
action_guidelines: [P1] When paginating through FHIR search results, reconstruct the next page URL exactly as provided in the 'next' link from the bundle, do not manually construct _getpages parameters.
[P1] Before creating orders or making clinical decisions, ensure all pages of search results have been fully retrieved and reviewed.
[P1] If a pagination request fails, retry using the exact 'next' link from the previous response bundle rather than manually constructing parameters.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent needed to find last flu vaccine date for a patient and order if >365 days ago. It searched for vaccine records, found none, correctly concluded no record existed, and ordered a new vaccine. Task succeeded.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent succeeded. No failure occurred; the absence of records was correctly interpreted as 'more than 365 days since last vaccine'.
action_guidelines: [P1] When searching for a vaccine date, query all immunization records without date filters first, then check if any exist.
[P1] If no vaccine records are found for a patient, treat the patient as not having received the vaccine within the required interval.
[P1] For time-based vaccine ordering rules, absence of any record implies the last dose was >365 days ago (or never given).
[P1] When ordering a vaccine based on a rule, use the current date as the order date.

action_guidelines: [P1] When searching for the most recent procedure, first query without date filters to find all instances, then identify the latest date.
[P1] If a procedure code search returns no results, remove date filters and retry before concluding absence.
[P1] After finding the most recent procedure date, compare against the 12-month threshold before ordering a new study.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent needed to verify active opioid orders for patient S3241217 and create naloxone if any found. Agent incorrectly concluded no active opioids existed, likely due to incomplete or filtered search. Task failed because agent did not exhaustively search all opioid categories or verify search parameters.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because it prematurely concluded no active opioid orders without confirming search completeness. Likely used overly narrow filters or failed to check all relevant opioid medication codes/names. Did not follow existing rule to broaden search before concluding absence.
action_guidelines: [P1] When checking for active opioid orders, search using a broad list of opioid names and codes without restrictive status or date filters first.
[P1] Before concluding no active opioids exist, verify the patient has at least one medication order of any kind to confirm search is working.
[P1] If initial opioid search returns zero results, retry with expanded code list (e.g., include generic names, brand names, and RxNorm codes) before concluding absence.
[P1] Document which specific opioid codes or names were searched to enable audit of search completeness.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent attempted to order a COVID-19 booster for a patient with no vaccination records found, but used an incorrect CPT code (91320) instead of a valid CVX or RxNorm code for the medication order, resulting in an invalid action.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent succeeded in searching for vaccination records across multiple resource types but failed because it used a CPT code (91320) for a MedicationRequest instead of a proper vaccine code from CVX or RxNorm, making the FHIR action invalid.
action_guidelines: [P1] When ordering a vaccine via MedicationRequest, use a CVX or RxNorm code for the medication, not a CPT or procedure code.
[P1] Verify that the code system used for medication orders matches FHIR requirements (e.g., CVX for vaccines, RxNorm for drugs).
[P1] If no prior vaccination records exist, confirm the patient is eligible for a booster based on age and risk factors before ordering.
